data.title: ZIM Integrated Shipping: Rich Income Yields Thanks To The Ongoing Uncertainties
data.description:
ZIM Remains Attractively Valued Despite The Recent Rally,

We previously covered ZIM Integrated Shipping (NYSE:ZIM) in March 2025, discussing why its sideways trading since May 2024 had been a boon, with it triggering the rich TTM dividend yields of 21.18%, thanks to FY2024 variable dividend payouts of $7.98 per share.

Thanks to the still elevated spot rates and contracted rates, the container company was likely to deliver another year of rich return profile in FY2025, significantly supported by the management's promising guidance, 40% of its capacity being LNG powered, and the potentially prolonged Red Sea conflict.

ZIM 1Y Stock Price

Since then, ZIM has already offered an excellent total return of +19.9% compared to the wider market at +4.5%, thanks to the rich dividend payout at $3.17 per share on April 03, 2025 and $0.74 per share on June 09, 2025.

Much of its tailwinds are naturally attributed to the elevated Drewry's World Container Index at a composite value of $3.54K per 40 feet containers as of June 12, 2025, up drastically from the pre-Red Sea attack levels of $1.36K on October 19, 2023 and the $1.4K average in 2019.

The same has been observed in ZIM's higher average freight rates per TEU of $1.77K in FQ1'25 (-5.8% QoQ/ +22% YoY) and $1.88K in FY2024 (+57% YoY), with the intensifying geopolitical conflicts and the ongoing tariff war likely to be top/ bottom-line accretive in the near-term.

This is significantly aided by the container company's higher carried volumes at 944K TEUs (-3.8% QoQ/ +11.5% YoY), with it underscoring the management's highly competent capacity/ cargo network allocation along with the fleet renewal cadence with lower cost base and the "larger, more modern, cost-effective capacity, approximately 40% of which is LNG-fueled."

These reasons are also why ZIM has been able to report higher total revenues of $2B (-7.4% QoQ/ +28.2% YoY), adj EBITDA margins of 38.8% (-5.8 points QoQ/ +11.5 YoY/ +27.2 from FY2019 levels of 11.6%), and net incomes of $296M (-47.4% QoQ/ +221.7% YoY), with the latter already triggering the richer dividend payouts.

This is based on the company's dividend policy at 30% of Q1/ Q2/ Q4 net incomes and 50% of Q4 net incomes, with the richer bottom-lines directly accretive to its ability to finance its shareholder return program.

We have also observed an improved balance sheet health, with ZIM reporting a lower net leverage ratio of 0.6x in FQ1'25 (compared to 0.8x a quarter ago and 2.8x a year ago) and a lower net debt level of -$2.49B (+13.5% QoQ/ +19.9% YoY).

This is despite the notably elevated lease liabilities of $4.53B (+22.1% QoQ/ -1.5% YoY) albeit at fixed rates and stretching through 2038, with it implying the company's ability to service its debt/ lease obligations.

It is apparent from these developments that ZIM has benefited from the ongoing macroeconomic uncertainties, allowing them to weather the containership capacity oversupply over the next few years.

These reasons may also be why the management has felt confident to reiterate their FY2025 guidance with adj EBITDA of between $1.6B and $2.2B (-48.5% at the midpoint), building upon the $779M reported in FQ1'25 (-19.4% QoQ/ +82.4% YoY).

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These may also be why the consensus forward estimates remain somewhat optimistic, with ZIM expected to remain profitable on an adj EBITDA and Free Cash Flow basis over the next few years.

These developments imply their ability to finance their future operations while offering a variable dividend payout depending on their lumpy net income profitability.

So, Is ZIM Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

ZIM 4Y Stock Price

For now, thanks to the rising Drewry's World Container Index spot values, we can understand why the ZIM stock has had a fast and furious rally by +49.5% since the recent April 2025 bottom as it also appears to be well supported at its 100 day moving averages.

As with most dividend stocks, the capital appreciation has directly triggered its moderating its forward dividend yields to 17.86%, compared to the average YTD yields of 39.32% and FY2024 levels of 53.15%.

Does this mean that ZIM is no longer attractive here?

Not quite.

As discussed in prior articles, the container shipping industry (including the dry bulk and commodity industries) is one that has been and will remain extremely volatile depending on the macroeconomic/ geopolitical environment.

Given the intensifying conflict between Israel/ Iran, we believe that the volatile spot rates will continue for a little longer, with it building upon the ongoing Israel/ Gaza conflict and the Ukraine/ Russia conflict.

Even so, we maintain our conclusion that the ZIM stock remains suitable for dividend oriented investors with a higher risk tolerance, since it continues to offer a relatively rich dividend yield of 17.86% compared to the sector median of 1.54%, A.P. Møller - Mærsk (OTCPK:AMKBY) (OTCPK:AMKAF) (OTCPK:AMKBF) at 8.66%, and the US Treasury Yields at between 3.95% and 4.89%.

Moving forward, we believe that there is an excellent visibility into its future top/ bottom-line performance and consequently, dividends paid out as well, since the management has opted to go for a 30% (spot market) - 70% (contract) split to temper the risks arising from the volatile spot values through April 2026.

At the same time, ZIM is already looking into ways to mitigate the upcoming port charges aimed at Chinese-built ships, as they actively shift "tonnage between trades to ensure that we minimize the effect of that fee on our cost structure and as a result avoid to having to incur incremental costs that we would need to at some point try to return," since "a bit less than half of it(s fleets) is Chinese-built."

This is on top of the likely expansion in its top/ bottom-line performance in FQ2'25, as more companies take advantage of the ongoing 90 days tariff pause before the deadline in early July 2025, with investors likely looking forward to a sequentially higher dividend payout out ahead.

As a result of the still robust dividend investment thesis, we are reiterating our Buy rating here.

data.title: Late Innings For The Gold Stock Rally
data.description:
The gold stock rally is near our long-standing upside target of HUI 500 +/-

Of course, a target is just a target. Not a stop sign. But while today’s bulls were cowering or chasing Nvidia and other sexy headline items back in January, we were discussing why the macro-fundamental picture was aligning positive for the gold mining industry.

I believe backing up my assertions, so as just one example, the January 26th edition of Notes From the Rabbit Hole (NFTRH 846) included this picture of the “last man standing” in the way of a purely bullish fundamental view of gold mining among other bullish signs for gold’s “real” price, as measured against cyclical markets.

Gold’s ratios to other markets continue to be constructive. This weekly chart gives a bigger picture view and it shows Gold/Commodities firmly trending up in what is disinflationary, counter-cyclical signaling. But it is not yet “deflation scare” crisis. It is being interpreted by bubble heads far and wide as “Goldilocks”… ah, just right! I continue to see the S&P 500 as the “last man standing” in the way of a solid counter-cyclical, possibly deflationary view.

Well, the last man standing (SPX) eventually cracked and the best of the rally ensued for the counter-cyclical gold stock sector. Also, from the summary segment of that January report:

Precious Metals: Gold broke out of its Symmetrical Triangle and above resistance. Hence, the long-standing target of 3000+ is loaded. Silver broke above the wedge (handle, bull flag) top and the 50 day average, nesting atop those markers currently. As long as it continues to do that or better, we are targeting 42. GDX needs to clear 38.72 and hold it to officially break the correction. If so, using HUI the next target is 375+ (current price: 305.86).

Gold went on to smash its target, silver has still not reached its target (since revised downward a bit, after the Cup & Handle pattern failed) and GDX obviously cleared 38.72 and broke its correction. HUI broke above 375, dropped to test and shake out the cling-ons, and then left that marker in the dust.

This brought on the current big picture target of 500 (+/-). HUI (currently 430) is only about 16% from the target zone, while leaving behind 115% of chart real estate from the 2024 lows and 145% from the bear market low in 2022.

Per the chart above, the fundamentals had actually been grinding positive since 2022 (neutral vs. stock markets). Here is the updated version of that chart. A wise investor or trader will tune out war/politics and inflation noise and focus on what matters; gold’s relationship to cyclical and more inflation sensitive markets. The situation has obviously improved, fundamentally.

Today we have a situation where the stock market’s relief rally (which we also anticipated quite well) has bent the fundamentals, but has been far from breaking them.

But there are signs of excess within the precious metals complex, internally. And again, we are only 16% from target. A target does not need to be a stop sign, but when you’ve had a target in place for years as an objective, it’s probably a good idea to respect it. NFTRH associate, Hammer (@joe_hammer_gb), who has cooked up quite an interesting cycle timing system, is looking for an end to this phase of the bull through completely different methods than my target. He has been appropriately bullish gold stocks, and has for some time viewed June as a timing window.

This public post is far from comprehensive. We will look at the sector’s internals, momentum and of course fundamentals, along with more technicals, as usual, going forward. But boiling it down to one chart – a chart I have used for years now, since the bull began in 2016 – it will pay to be conscious of where we are vs. where we have been, and the once-future expectations that are now only 16% away. It’s a risk/reward thing.

data.title: Geopolitical Tensions + U.S. Political Volatility Raise The Risk Profile (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Market Analysis - Nasdaq - (NASDAQ:QQQ)

I wrote last week that I was starting to see some warning signs and was increasing my risk posture. I noticed that leading stocks were starting to falter and lack power, that the market was in an upward trending wedge channel, a market pattern that typically resolves to the downside, some yellow flags in the volume profiles, and that political risks remained.

It's a little too soon to declare victory on that one, but the market did end up resolving the wedge pattern to the downside. This occurred Friday, on the back of rising Middle East tensions as Israel performed a preemptive strike on Iran's nuclear facilities and other targets, while Iran responded by bombing Israeli cities. While we will focus the rest of this article on the market, I'll say unequivocally that I stand with Israel and think the entire world owes Israel a debt of gratitude for its role as the tip of the spear against Islamic extremism.

While we did resolve the wedge pattern to the downside, the technician in me wasn't exactly floored by the market's behavior on Friday. You'll note a moderate gap down - a little over 1% - which was bought up for most of the day until Iran's response struck Israel. The market close near the lows, although the volume was not overwhelming. In my mind, this wasn't a "sell signal" kind of day... but it's another yellow or maybe orange flag.

The volume profiles continue to say that "distribution" is underway. Up days are low volume, down days are heavier volume, and the only 3 days in the last several weeks with above average volume were all red (see chart).

From a technical perspective, I continue to think a retest of the 21 EMA (teal line) at minimum is likely. A retest of the 50/100/200 SMAs - all of which are currently or will soon be in the region of that ~393-500 bullish gap - also seems on the table.

If you're looking for hot geopolitical takes, you'll have to head elsewhere, but, briefly: an all out war between Israel and Iran would be bad for the market. A regional war throughout the Middle East would be really bad for the market. And a worldwide extension of said war would be terrible for the market.

None of that is necessarily going to happen, but, it should be crystal clear from this week's events that the dispersion of near term outcomes has widened significantly. Oh, and the trade issues and Trump Administration volatility haven't exactly gone away. Risks are higher today than they were a week ago, and as momentum traders, we need to act accordingly.

So, on balance, I'm going to stick with last week's call that short term risks appear to be increasing, that we are due for a pullback, and that, as a result, I am heightening my risk posture. I do continue to think that in the intermediate term, all time highs remain in sight.

Gold (GLD)

You'll recall that last week I wrote of having picked up a position in gold at the fingerpoint and was trying to be patient and let it work for me. I was certainly helped by the recent events in the Middle East. I'd rather be lucky than good, but I'd really rather Iran wasn't developing into a potential world ending threat.

I'll trend this one against the moving averages and think all time highs are in sight. I tend to take fairly large positions in gold as it moves slowly, but if the market moves down and stops me out of my stocks, I would probably be willing to buy an add on position if it consolidates in the realm of all time highs.

Bitcoin (BTC-USD)

Bitcoin has been steadily setting more and more of a pattern after topping out around 112k. It looks like it's inclined to respect the 50 SMA (dark blue line) but has downtrending resistance overhead. To my eyes, this pattern is more likely than not to resolve higher from where we are sitting here, but it's going to need to flesh out a little better for me to take a long side trading position.

If we lose the 50, the downside risks increase, although the rising 100 and 200 SMAs (grey and orange lines respectively) are there for support underneath.

Interestingly, you'll note that Bitcoin has diverged from gold's performance as the tensions have risen in the Middle East over the last few days, Bitcoin chopping lower, Gold pushing out higher. I do, to some degree, buy the "digital gold" narrative and think it will increasingly be seen that way over the long run... but that day is not today. You can tell what the real "flight to safety" asset is.

(PS my favorite bold call: Bitcoin will one day be the world's reserve currency.)

Oil

I'm not exactly breaking news on this one, but oil had an enormous day following the Israel/Iran strike and counterstrike. It actually closed materially off the highs, but this is a pretty big change in character on the weekly chart below. Oil challenged the ~67 level many times over the last few years and appeared to break down before shooting back up. Obviously oils price will be a reflection of Middle East tensions for the foreseeable future.

I'll plan to look at some oil stocks in the coming weeks for possible momentum trades but don't want to make any big decisions off a single big day. These event trades have such a significant possibility of fading if the event is already priced in, or if the event does not proceed as commodity traders expect. I'd rather wait, watch for oil stocks to show us which names are strongest, then buy a consolidation rather than lose money trying to be first.

Portfolio

I ended up selling CoreWeave (CRWV) up about 220% for two reasons. First, past a certain point you've "gotten what you came for." Second, I'm looking to raise a little cash as the stock market's risk profile increases.

I tried a few new names over the last week but haven't really gotten much to stick (looking at you Shopify (SHOP) and Credo (CRDO)), another sign that we may be looking at a pullback in the short term.

Longs include Aeva Technologies (AEVA) (up 205%, first discussed here), AST SpaceMobile (ASTS), TransMedics (TMDX), Oklo (OKLO), CrowdStrike (CRWD), MicroVast (MVST), Porch (PRCH), RobinHood (HOOD), Tempus AI (TEM), and Palantir (PLTR).

AST SpaceMobile (ASTS)

I will be the first to tell you that I really hate trading this thing. It is incredibly whippy and choppy. When trading momentum, the best stocks are the ones that make smooth moves rather than chopping around. Stocks like ASTS tend to chop and wiggle, stopping you out in what turns out to have been a fake move before proceeding higher without you.

So why did I trade it?

Well, this thing really moves when it wants to, and as a "space" company, it fits squarely within one of the market's most dominant themes.

Frankly I'm almost embarrassed to show you this chart because the supply/demand level I bought through is so ill-defined you might think less of me - but that's just the nature of this stock!

ASTS has been the beneficiary of a lot of good news lately, first with the June 4th buzz that Jeff Bezos and his Blue Origin may be involved in some way. Second, the company is set to join the Russell 1000. And, third, the company secured mid band spectrum for direct to device service in North America.

Taking a look at the chart, you can see ASTS made a really nice move in the summer of last year and has been chopping more or less sideways since. When looking for momentum stocks, you want to see sideways consolidation without give back, and... after the give back from the parabolic portion, I think you could argue we see that. It chopped into the 200 SMA (orange line), supported, tried to break out, failed, found support on the 200 SMA again, before tightening up over on that right side again and subsequently breaking out on volume.

I'm sitting up about 45% on this one and will plan to trend against moving averages. In the ideal world, this would chop out a flag pattern as we sit somewhere around all time highs, allowing an add-on buy and second breakout.

A last note on this one: I always keep an eye on darlings of recent market cycles, because maybe about half the time they have another big leg left in them. This is especially true if they don't completely fall apart but are able to hold the 200 SMA in particular. You can contrast that with something like Super Micro Computer (SMCI), another recent darling, which is, to me, dead and gone and forgotten, having gone parabolic and completely crapped out through the 200 SMA and is now basing comfortably below it. With that kind of action, I'd need to see it start a whole new uptrend before I'd get interested:

Watch List

As noted, I am raising my risk profile. I will be cautious with new positions and give them a short leash, and I may pass on second tier stock setups altogether. That said, if the market does go through a pullback period, this is exactly when you want to be monitoring closely for future winners. Here is some of what I am watching today:

StoneCo (STNE)

STNE has enjoyed a hype cycle or two in the past, when Buffett/Berkshire had previously invested in the Brazil payments company, and the stock went fully parabolic in the 2020-2021 bubble market.

STNE has all the hallmarks of the type of company I like to trade and have highlighted a handful of times: completely bombed out, left for dead and forgotten, until... it wakes up. Reports are that the company is expanding into new markets and has put up double digit revenue and earnings growth.

We can start with the weekly chart, and you can see the significance of the ~15 level (lower of the two grey lines) that has capped all rallies - except one - since it was a high flyer in the 2020-2021 bubble market.

The current base looks very clean as well with a clear supply/demand level and very little give back after a ~90% run up while allowing the 50 SMA (dark blue line) to catch up:

But it's not all roses: STNE previously tried a similar breakout in July of 2023 but got stuffed not long after pushing through the 15 level we've discussed (see weekly chart above), so my radar is a little higher on this one that we could see a similar failure.

Rubrik (RBRK)

If dead and forgotten stocks are among my favorite to play, recent IPOs is probably the other major category. These are stocks that can really run when they get some buzz behind them.

RBRK was actually a winner last year after coming out of its first IPO base, moving up about 100% from the optimal buy point. Since then, it put in a double bottom base and then rocketed out without ever really giving an attractive buy point.

I've been watching this one closely, and it looks like earnings may have finally given us an opportunity to get involved. The report was not well received. The stock traded down about 15% off the highs on concerns related to dilution and a convertible note. While that is understandable, from a momentum perspective, I am more interested in the ~50% YoY revenue growth and earnings beat along with the raised guidance.

I'd like to see this chop around over the next couple of weeks and set a more clear supply demand level. If it can do so, this will certainly be on the list.

Conclusion

We were already in a somewhat politically volatile situation with the unpredictable Trump and the potential for tariffs to create real economic pain, but now geopolitical issues in the Middle East have raised the risk profile further.

With the dispersion of short term outcomes widening and the market throwing up some yellow, orange, and even red flags, I am raising my risk profile yet further and generally expect a pullback in the short term.

data.title: 5 Closed-End Fund Buys In The Month Of May 2025
data.description:
With a rapid recovery from April's low as we went through May, there weren't as many opportunities for adding aggressively as there were. I let a bit more cash build up in the latest month after dipping into my cash pool in April's market correction. That said, every single month, I reinvest a portion of the cash flow received into my closed-end fund account, as well as being able to contribute outside funds. That means I'm building up a snowball of cash flow month after month and compounding over time.

#1 Blackstone Strategic Credit 2027 Term Fund (BGB)

BGB is a fund I had owned at one point and sold out of it, but now I'm back in. The fund is one that is primarily invested in floating-rate-based assets. So, with 100 basis points of cuts from the Fed already in and projections for further reductions in the future, there will be pressure on this fund's distribution. They adjust the distribution amount each quarter, and so seeing some variability is expected.

However, the more attractive characteristic of the fund is the term structure and seeing the fund's discount has widened out more recently. I scooped up shares when it touched around the 6% discount level, which is similar to where it is today.

We covered the fund recently, but the main idea is that with the fund's term structure, it is anticipated to liquidate on September 15, 2027. That provides a way for the fund's discount to be realized and generate alpha. However, as always, there are caveats to consider:

With the above said, it is important to understand that there is an incentive for the fund to continue existing for the fund sponsor, and to continue providing those fees. They can choose to put it up to a vote to become a perpetual fund, which can sometimes be accompanied by a tender offer for 100% of shares at 100% of NAV per share at that time.

This fund was launched prior to that becoming standard language in the CEF 2.0 model, so the prospectus doesn't specifically state it. However, we've seen other Nuveen funds go this route anyway, without that language in their prospectus either. An example of that would be the Nuveen Preferred Securities & Income Opportunities Fund (JPI).

There have been a select couple of other funds that just put it up to a vote to go perpetual, which saw approval. However, those funds were regularly trading at premiums, and it would then make sense to approve the perpetual structure. Going that route included XAI Octagon Floating Rate & Alternative Income Trust (XFLT) and Western Asset Mortgage Opportunity Fund (DMO).

In either scenario, it made sense to switch to perpetual, and there was still a way for investors to offload their holdings at or above 100% of NAV.

A further caveat is, the Board, at its sole discretion (without shareholder approval), can extend the term for up to 2 years, or a "shorter time as may be determined." Extending the term can be beneficial if the market is in a slump and the Board has reason to believe that postponing the liquidation will result in NAV recovery. Therefore, essentially gets a better liquidation price for shareholders.

#2 ArrowMark Financial Corp (BANX)

BANX is another floating-rate debt-focused investing fund, more specifically investing in regulatory capital relief securities. These are opaque investments but have been delivering consistently strong cash flow. While the Fed cutting its target rate will put pressure on the income generation of the fund, the distribution coverage on this one is quite strong at 125% based on the last reported quarterly NII. Therefore, I suspect that the distribution won't be reduced soon, as there is quite a bit of cushion—of course, there are no guarantees. They've been paying out specials as well due to the strong level of coverage.

We also covered this fund more recently. Based on the last reported estimated NAV of $21.74 at the end of April 30, 2025, we'd see the fund's discount at 7.82%. This is well off the over 20% discount level it touched when I originally added to this position, but it remains a fairly attractive level for entry anyway. One of the reasons I added to this position was that since I originally added it, my allocation fell to below 1% of my portfolio. This was due to adding to other positions over time

#3 BlackRock Science and Technology Term Trust (BSTZ) And #4 BlackRock Enhanced Global Dividend Trust (BOE)

I'm lumping these two together due to them being the result of the latest round of tender offers based on the discount management program. With this discount management program, the funds had to have a discount average greater than 7.5% during the measurement period of January 1 to March 31, 2025. The discounts have been narrowing on these funds, so with this round, I only had BSTZ and BOE that hit the trigger level.

For these tender offers, they were offering to repurchase 2.5% of outstanding shares at 98% of NAV. Of course, 2.5% is a very small amount, but what makes it more worthwhile is that not everyone participates. This could be due to not being aware of the tender offer, not being sure how to participate, or believing that it isn't worthwhile.

So what ends up happening is that investors who do participate end up having a significantly larger portion of their shares accepted. For BOE, they had a pro-ration factor of 0.11614930—in other words, the fund accepted 11.61% from investors who tendered 100% of their position. The purchase price was $11.8776, and I added back the position and some more at $11.34. The fund's discount has been narrowing significantly over the last year, but it is still at a level where I feel comfortable adding even more.

BSTZ similarly saw a high pro-ration factor; in fact, it was even higher at 0.13876393. The purchase price on this one was $20.0018, and I added the shares back and more at $19.27. Similar to BOE, BSTZ has seen its discount narrow meaningfully over the last year or so. However, as tech remains in a bit of a slump off its peak in February 2025, I also felt comfortable adding more to this position.

Going forward, there is a new discount management program that was implemented for 2025. It isn't as generous, as the discount of these funds would have to average more than 10% during a 9-month measurement period between January 1 to September 30, 2025. However, if that occurs, the fund will repurchase a "minimum of 5% of its outstanding common shares."

#5 Eagle Point Income Co (EIC)

Finally, I added to EIC on the back of the plunge that the fund saw with the announcement of cutting their distribution from $0.20 per month to $0.13. As another floating-rate-based fund, they've finally made the decision to cut their payout based on declining NII.

This was similar to XFLT cutting their distribution earlier. EIC's sister fund, Eagle Point Credit Co (ECC), had also stopped the supplemental in 2025 that they had been paying out every single month previously. I wasn't necessarily specifically looking to add so much more floating-rate exposure to my portfolio last month, but I took the opportunity on EIC as it was presented.

data.title: The Middleby Corporation's Separation May Fuel Upside
data.description:
Over a year ago, in early April 2024, I made the claim that continued growth, as well as the low share price that it boasted, made The Middleby Corporation (NASDAQ:MIDD) an attractive opportunity for investors. Since then, some things have changed. The 2024 fiscal year was not particularly pleasant for shareholders. And this year, we have seen mixed results, with revenue actually continuing its decline. There are certain broader economic challenges as well that need to be factored in. Even so, I believe that this still represents an appealing prospect. It is certainly not the best candidate on the market. But considering how cheap shares are and a major catalyst that management announced earlier this year, I believe that The Middleby Corporation is certainly worthy of a bullish assessment.

An interesting firm

Recent financial performance achieved by The Middleby Corporation has not been the best. After seeing revenue inch up ever so slightly from $4.03 billion in 2022 to $4.04 billion in 2023, there was a pretty big drop to $3.88 billion last year. This pain for the company came from two of its three operating segments. The big driver was the Commercial Foodservice Equipment Group segment, which provides food service equipment such as different types of ovens, fryers, steam cooking equipment, heated cabinets, charbroilers, induction cooking equipment, charcoal grills, and more. Year over year, revenue for this segment contracted from $2.52 billion to $2.42 billion. Most of this was domestic organic weakness for the company, with revenue falling about 6.5%, with management attributing that to slow market conditions.

Though not as painful, the company also saw weakness when it came to the Residential Kitchen Equipment Group segment, with revenue dropping from $794.5 million to $724.9 million. Had it not been for foreign currency fluctuations, the drop in revenue would have been a bit worse. Management described market conditions as being challenged, both domestically and in the firm's European markets. The only area of strength for the business from 2023 to 2024 was the Food Processing Equipment Group, which reported a rise from $720.6 million to $731 million. That can be attributed to acquisitions that added $24.7 million to the top line. Without this, revenue would have dropped as well, primarily because of lower sales volumes of protein products in the domestic market.

With revenue dropping, you would expect profitability to fall as well. But actually, net income rose from $400.9 million in 2023 to $428.4 million last year. Other profitability metrics have been rather mixed. After skyrocketing from $332.6 million in 2022 to $628.8 million in 2023, operating cash flow for the business rose further to $686.8 million last year. On an adjusted basis, there was a small decline from $652.7 million in 2023 to $650.9 million last year. Similarly, EBITDA for the business contracted from $900.4 million to $866.3 million.

For the first quarter of 2025, investors have seen continued challenges. Revenue dropped to $906.6 million compared to the $926.9 million the business reported the previous year. While the Residential Kitchen Equipment Group segment for the company reported a slight rise in revenue from $173.9 million to $176 million, with management attributing that to greater growth in the outdoor products category in the domestic market that was offset somewhat by weakness in its European market, the business did see a decline associated with its Commercial Foodservice Equipment Group from $581.4 million to $562.7 million.

That drop was because of slower market conditions that related mostly to lower chain customer store traffic and lower replacement demand. Weakness in the Latin American market also impacted sales. Meanwhile, the Food Processing Equipment Group reported a drop from $171.6 million to $167.9 million. Lower sales volumes in both the protein and bakery products categories in the US, as well as slow market conditions in certain Asian markets within the bakery group, negatively affected this segment.

Even though revenue declined, net income actually improved slightly, from $86.6 million to $92.4 million. The good news was that the company benefited from a pretty nice decline in interest expense and deferred financing amortization from $26.3 million to $18.4 million. It also reported slightly lower restructuring costs year over year. Operating cash flow grew from $140.9 million to $141.1 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a fall from $123.5 million to $118.9 million. Lastly, EBITDA for the business dropped from $185.8 million to $182.1 million.

When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year as a whole, we don't really know what to expect. Management did warn investors that, as things stood at the time that they announced results for the first quarter of the year, they were expecting annual expenses to rise by between $150 million and $200 million because of tariffs. The good news is that management said that price increases that it will put on to its customers, as well as certain operational measures, should cover this pain. But even so, this doesn't mean that consumers will accept any price increases that they might need to swallow. So there is uncertainty here.

If we use the historical results that the company achieved for the last two completed fiscal years, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. These are not high multiples, but they certainly aren't the lowest. Having said that, it is worth noting that, relative to other similar companies, shares are near the cheaper end of the spectrum. In the table below, I stacked the company up against five similar enterprises. And what I found was that, on both a price to earnings basis and on an EV to EBITDA basis, only one of the five companies was cheaper than our prospect is. And on a price to operating cash flow basis, it was the cheapest of the group.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
The Middleby Corporation 18.5 12.2 11.0
Watts Water Technologies (WTS) 27.8 21.9 17.9
Donaldson Company (DCI) 20.4 19.8 13.2
ESAB Corporation (ESAB) 27.9 21.9 16.2
Mueller Industries (MLI) 14.2 15.1 8.7
Chart Industries (GTLS) 32.1 13.7 12.4

In addition to being cheap on a relative basis, The Middleby Corporation also has an interesting catalyst that could create some attractive upside for shareholders. In January of this year, management announced that the company had been conducting a strategic review of its business portfolio that began last year. This was based on management's belief that there was unrealized value in the company's portfolio that could be extracted in a way that would be beneficial for shareholders. On February 25th, management then announced that they had decided to spin off the Food Processing Equipment Group of the enterprise into a separate publicly traded company. Spinoffs can create risks for shareholders. But they can also open the door to greater upside. Anybody who has followed General Electric for a long time can attest to that.

As the charts throughout this article have demonstrated, the Food Processing Equipment Group part of the enterprise has done quite well in recent years. It has achieved faster growth than the other parts of the company. Those other parts our market leading brands in their spaces. And management does believe that there is upside for further growth for them. However, the different capital structure that will exist between the two separate companies, as well as a strong mergers and acquisitions pipeline and other actionable initiatives for the Food Processing Equipment Group should create greater potential value as time goes on.

The company has a long history of growing the Food Processing Equipment Group by means of acquisition. In fact, since 2005, the segment has allocated around $750 million toward buying up other businesses. In that time, it has purchased 31 new businesses. And management believes that there remains a pipeline of around 35 targets that could bring on around $600 million worth of annual EBITDA to that enterprise.

If everything goes according to plan, this standalone company will have the potential to grow revenue from the $731 million it generated last year to $1 billion by 2028. A rise in the EBITDA margin for the business, combined with the rise in revenue, should push annual EBITDA up to around $270 million compared to the $187.3 million it generated last year.

There's also opportunity for the rest of the business. Through continued strategic investments, such as the eight innovation centers that the company has already built, management believes that what is left of The Middleby Corporation will also have the opportunity to expand. With revenue for the Residential Kitchen Equipment Group expected to rise at a high single digit rate and revenue for the Commercial Foodservice Equipment Group anticipated to grow at a mid-single digit rate, management is pushing for sales of the combined company to hit $4 billion by 2028. That compares to the $3.14 billion reported the previous year. Should it hit those targets, management believes that EBITDA should climb to roughly $1 billion by the end of that forecast period.

If we assume that both of these companies can hit their targets by 2028, and if we apply the same EV to EBITDA multiple to the two firms individually and also assume that the overall net debt of the two businesses combined will not change, then investors would be able to expect annualized upside between now and the end of 2028 of about 13.2%. Considering that the S&P 500 averages around 11% to 12% per year, that's not bad. Having said that, I think a case could be made that the expected margin expansion of these firms would justify slightly higher trading multiples than what the company has at the moment. If instead of using an EV to EBITDA multiple of 11, we used a multiple of 12, the annualized upside would be 16.3%. And if we increase that to 13, we would get an upside of 19.3% per annum.

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I must say that I remain slightly bullish about The Middleby Corporation. The company has faced some setbacks recently. But its overall health is solid and shares seem to offer upside, especially if current projections by management over the next few years turn out to be correct. There is obviously some uncertainty, particularly when it comes to the tariff situation and how consumers will respond. But outside of that, I think that the business is worthy of a ‘buy’ rating at this time.

data.title: Merchants Bancorp: Shifting Away From The Common Shares To Its Newest Preferred Share
data.description:
Introduction

Merchants Bancorp (NASDAQ:MBIN) is an Indiana based regional bank. In addition to offering common shares, the bank also has three different preferred share issuances (MBINM) (NASDAQ:MBINN) (NASDAQ:MBINL). I wrote about Merchants back in November and was bullish about the common shares at the time. Since that article, one preferred share has been called, a new preferred share has been issued, and the bank’s loan performance has deteriorated. As a result, I’ve downgraded the common shares to a hold and suggest that income investors purchase the long-dated preferred shares (MBINL).

Merchants Bancorp Financial Results

Merchants Bancorp has ridden the wild ride of interest rates over the last five years. At the lowest rate levels of the pandemic, the bank saw its asset yield sit at around 3%, only to jump to nearly 8% by the first quarter of 2024. Borrowing yields went from under 0.5% to over 5% in the same period. Over the last few quarters, asset yields have eased to under 7% as the Fed has lowered interest rates and borrowing yields have dropped to 4.41%. Despite the interest rate volatility, net interest spread, and net interest margins have remained stable over the last five years.

From an income and expense standpoint, Merchants Bancorp’s results have moved with interest rates. Interest income went from under $100 million to nearly $350 million on a quarterly basis after interest rates rose and fell to $287 million in the first quarter. Interest expense went from under $10 million to over $200 million but also eased down to $165 million in the first quarter. Net interest income (interest income less interest expense) dropped by $12 million to $122 million for the first quarter, the first drop in net interest income in three years signaling that lower rates may not be beneficial for the bank’s financial results.

Changes in Loans and Deposits

After several quarters of robust loan growth for Merchants Bancorp, lending has been slow in 2024 and 2025. In the first quarter, lending grew at 0.3% for the quarter and was down 0.2% year over year. Loan growth has been under 1% for four consecutive quarters. Meanwhile, deposits grew by more than 6% in the first quarter, after two consecutive quarters of large declines. Deposits are still down by more than 11% on a year-over-year basis.

The large drop in deposits has caused the bank’s loan to deposit ratio to jump to uncomfortable levels. After dropping below 100% in early 2024, the ratio surged to over 120% in the fourth quarter and is currently at 114%. A high loan to deposit ratio means that the bank needs other means of external financing (other than deposits) to finance loan growth. Merchants Bancorp has more than tripled its level of external financing in the last year, which is a more expensive form of financing and will negatively impact future net interest income.

The Loan Performance Risk

Like many other regional banks, loan performance is the main concern, and the trend is very concerning with Merchants Bancorp. After a relatively calm period, non-performing loans shot up to encompass nearly 2% of the total loan portfolio as of the end of the first quarter. Fortunately, the parabolic trend seems to have stalled, but the bank’s allowance for credit losses currently stands at 1.1% of gross loans, which is inadequate to handle this type of performance. The bank’s earnings appear poised to suffer, which is why I have downgraded the common shares.

Why MBINL over the Others?

Merchants Bancorp has three different preferred shares. One issue (MBINN) is trading at a discount due to a lower dividend, but its yield is the lowest of the group at 7.62%. The second highest yielding preferred share is MBINM at 8.2%. This issue does have the ability to reset for five years in October 2027, and at today’s rates, the dividend yield would rise to 8.4%, but is it worth 20 basis points to take that level of interest rate and duration risk? This is why I like MBINL, which is trading at an 8.25% yield, is fixed, and not callable until January 2030. MBINL can provide income investors with excellent yields no matter the interest rate trends.

Conclusion

While Merchant Bancorp’s performance is a bit scary, the bank is still able to fund the preferred dividends. The bank continues to pay a common dividend, but I am less confident in that holding up. The bank should not discontinue preferred dividends unless it comes under extreme stress, and I am currently not seeing that with deposit growth in the first quarter, flat loan growth, and a declining loan to deposit ratio. As investors wait for the bank to stabilize, they can enjoy a good income yield with the bank’s Series E preferred shares.

data.title: Celsius Holdings: Alani Nu Acquisition Masking Growth Challenges (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
As most investors know, any attempt of timing the market is one that is doomed to fail.

Well, in my coverage of Celsius Holdings (NASDAQ:CELH), I have to say my timing has been almost perfect, and while that's probably just a coincidence, I'm going to make another move at it.

I upgraded Celsius at the beginning of 2025, claiming it has reached valuation levels that provide a highly attractive floor ahead of a potential turnaround.

The stock is up nearly 50% since, and in my view, investors should trim or exit their positions altogether here.

Let's dive in.

Introduction To Celsius Holdings

On August 1st, 2022, Celsius Holdings announced a distribution agreement with PepsiCo (PEP). About 18 months later, Celsius' shares were among the best performers in the market, rising over 170%, whilst revenues more than doubled.

After reaching that top in mid-2024, Celsius reported a shockingly disappointing quarter, primarily due to inventory realignment done by PepsiCo. In the quarters that followed, the lingering effect of PepsiCo's changes, combined with an industry slowdown, and a deterioration in the hype around the Celsius brand, have all combined to result in three consecutive quarters of revenue declines.

Celsius shares have seen steep declines from that point on until early 2025. As the new year began, it became clearer that management is now operating under a new reality, and valuation became low enough to create a bottom.

Shares are up about 50% year-to-date, benefitting from the decision to acquire Alani Nu, one of the fastest growing energy brands.

In light of this sharp rise, the question that arises is what should investors do now?

First Quarter Results Tell The Story Of Persisting Struggles

Looking at Celsius' first quarter results, you'll have a hard time finding something to lean on that justifies a 50% increase in shares.

Revenue declined 7%, including a 10% decline in North America. While gross margins improved, operating margin contracted by an astounding 760 bps, leading to a 38% decline in operating profit. Similarly, net income was cut by 43%.

The Celsius stand-alone brand continues to see market share losses, as both Monster (MNST) and Red Bull have finally "answered the call" and expanded their no-sugar or low-sugar offerings.

Alani nu continues to perform well, but those who have been following the industry for a while have learned the painful lesson that the early market share gains are not representative of what the future will hold.

These energy brands tend to be exciting for a while, but over time, the legacy brands in Monster and Red Bull are those that stick and hold their dominance.

Alani Nu Acquisition Is Masking Problems, Sending Share Higher

As noted above, Alani Nu's growth continues to be strong, and somewhat resembles what we saw from Celsius two years ago, as it was able to overcome the industry's slowdown and grow rapidly.

Looking ahead, management continues to speak in numbers that aren't directly correlated with the company's P&L, which gives me pause.

For instance, from the recent earnings call:

Together, Celsius and Alani Nu accounted for approximately 20% of total energy drink category dollar growth in the first quarter of 2025, following a strong 50% contribution to total category growth in 2024.

...

We expect to continue gaining incremental space at retail, helping to drive greater consumer awareness and flavor availability, including recent innovation.

At the end of the day, if it wasn't for the Alani Nu acquisition, Celsius would have been a 56x P/E stock with declining revenues. Here's the math behind this claim:

That said, Celsius did acquire Alani Nu, so we should look at the combined entity before reaching a conclusion.

Valuation & Prospects

Alani Nu is set to be consolidated into Celsius results beginning April 1st, the day of the completion. This means that consensus estimates for pro-forma growth are only relevant from Q2-25 and beyond, which will be the first quarter the consolidation begins to lap.

Right now, consensus sees revenue growth at a high-single digit rate from that point on. Meanwhile, peers like Monster are expected to get back to high-single-digit growth starting next quarter.

Of note, Celsius is trading at a 43% discount to Monster based on EV to Revenues, in contrast to a 17% premium based on P/E.

The two main things that worry me, in the near term, is that there is an expectation that Alani Nu will continue to grow rapidly under the Celsius umbrella and despite reaching a market share where things tend to become tougher.

In addition, a 51x forward P/E is predicated on maintaining margin levels that are due to be diluted by the Alani Nu consolidation.

Both of these expectations lean to the very optimistic side, in my view. Meanwhile, there are significant risks to growth, especially as we're seeing competitive pressures increasing, and consumer spending contracting.

Conclusion

Celsius entered the year at valuation levels that reflected a company that will never recover. Six months later, shares are up 50%, trading at a 51x forward P/E, with consensus expecting Alani Nu growth to continue, Celsius growth to recover, on top of stable margins.

To me, this setup creates significant downside risks, mandating a downgrade to 'Hold'.

data.title: Ziff Davis: Too Cheap At 4 Times FCF Despite Its Risks
data.description:
Investment thesis

Ziff Davis (NASDAQ:ZD) is a media company which owns a number of websites in multiple categories, along with a cybersecurity and marketing related SaaS offering for businesses and consumers. This diversity makes ZD a complex business to comprehend, which when coupled with the risks tf faces due to AI and search, has led to the share price falling by nearly 80% since its peak in August 2021. The negative sentiment from investors is reflected in the current valuation, with shares trading at just 4 times FCF. The company nevertheless continues to generate strong FCF which is being deployed towards accretive acquisitions and buybacks, which should support solid EPS growth going forward. Despite the risks which I will walk through in this writeup, I find that ZD stock represents a compelling investment at this valuation.

Cheap valuation reflects underlying risks and concerns

As highlighted in the image above, ZD's management has guided for $523 million in Adjusted EBITDA for FY25 at the midpoint. My estimate for FCF stands at $328 million, after deducting $35 million and $50 million in interest and tax expenses respectively, along with $110 million in capex. At the current market cap of $1.3 billion, this puts the Price to FCF multiple at just 4, implying a FCF yield of 25%. If we deduct annual stock based compensation ("SBC") expenses of around $45 million in order to account for the negative impact from potential dilution, FCF minus SBC ends up at $283 million, with the FCF multiple only slightly increasing to 4.6.

The current valuation is very cheap given ZD's high gross margins of over 85% and adjusted EBITDA margins of close to 35%, while the business also needs limited capex for growth. While there are no direct public peers to compare its valuation against, I would point to IAC (IAC), a company I covered recently and owns the media publishing company Dotdash Meredith. When looking through its sum of the parts, I recognized that Dotdash Meredith was also being valued at roughly a mid single digit FCF multiple.

The biggest risk I believe that is keeping investors away is related to the threat from AI with respect to content creation and the move away from search to AI overviews by Google (GOOG). However in ZD's case, the fact that they own leading websites such as CNET, PCMag and IGN puts them in a stronger position compared to their peers. At a recent investor conference, addressing these concerns its CEO highlighted that less than 15% of its revenues depended on search and that its sites remained at the top of AI overviews. Nevertheless there is added uncertainty surrounding the recent lawsuit that the company filed against OpenAI for copyright infringement.

Another overhang on the stock is its high debt load, which stood at $872 million at the end of Q1, as shown above. Given that it also holds cash and investments totalling $598 million, its financial position is solid, with a net debt to Adjusted EBITDA ratio of 0.6. However with around half of this debt coming due in the next 3 years, its highly likely that this debt will have to be re-financed at much higher interest rates than the 1.75% and 3.63% interest rates on the 2026 and 2028 convertible notes. Notably, the $460 million in senior notes which are due only in 2030 carry an interest rate of 4.63%. While the potentially higher interest expenses represent a potential headwind to future FCF generation, I do not expect this to have a significant impact.

Turning to the business performance, we see that ZD's two largest publishing segments related to Technology and Heath and Wellness posted year over year growth of 17.9% and 7.3% respectively in Q1. On the flipside, subscription and licensing revenue related to its cybersecurity and marketing products saw a revenue decline of 10.8%. This led to overall revenue including acquisitions growing 4.5% in Q1, with Adjusted EBITDA remaining flat versus the prior year period at $100.2 million.

While revenue growth going forward should remain in the low to mid single digit range, depending on the contribution from acquisitions, its heavy dependency on advertising and marketing spend by business, does make it sensitive to macroeconomic conditions.

Analyzing the drivers to unlock upside potential

I think investors in ZD at this valuation stand to benefit from a combination of earnings growth and a multiple expansion. While posting consistent organic growth with steady margins should alleviate concerns related to the threat from AI, I believe management has the opportunity to enhance growth by leveraging the $431 million in cash on the balance sheet. Two acquisitions were completed in Q1, with management targeting a 20% cash on cash return profile. Meanwhile they have continued to deploy cash aggressively towards repurchases, with $35 million spent on buybacks in Q1. ZD's shares outstanding has shrunk by 9% over the past year.

A favorable resolution related to the OpenAI lawsuit should also benefit ZD, especially if ZD can turn this into an opportunity to earn additional licensing revenue from OpenAI similar to the strategic partnership between Dotdash Meredith and OpenAI.

Conclusion

While I recognize the risks associated with the business, especially due to AI, I think the current valuation at just 4 times FCF reflects too much pessimism. The debt load appears manageable and the strong FCF generation supports capital allocation towards accretive acquisitions and share buybacks. As a result, I am assigning a Buy rating for ZD stock.

data.title: Rigetti: Moonshot Physics Meets Market Math
data.description:
Intro

Rigetti Computing (NASDAQ:RGTI) sits at the intersection of frontier physics and public‑equity scrutiny. After a meteoric rally earlier in 2025, the stock now trades in the low‑double‑digits, giving the Berkeley‑based quantum pioneer a roughly $3.7 billion market value despite generating less than $10 million of annual revenue. That disconnect captures the essence of the story: investors are paying up today for breakthroughs that may not translate into durable cash flows for years.

Although quantum hype has cooled from the fever pitch of 2021, Rigetti’s recent demonstrations of error‑mitigation on its 84‑qubit Ankaa‑2 and the launch of the higher‑fidelity Ankaa‑3 show real scientific momentum. At the same time, first‑quarter financials underscored just how far commercialization still lags: revenue fell 52% year over year to $1.5 million while operating expenses climbed to $22.1 million.

Against that backdrop, this article adopts a neutral stance. The upside of a genuine technology inflection is balanced by execution risk, funding dilution, and a competitive landscape teeming with better‑capitalized rivals. Investors who can stomach binary outcomes may find optionality attractive, but patience remains the most valuable commodity in quantum.

Thesis

Rigetti threads a narrow path between moon‑shot ambition and harsh commercial reality. Its hybrid superconducting‑qubit architecture continues to notch benchmark improvements (median two‑qubit fidelity on Ankaa‑3 reached 98%, a 2.5x jump over the prior generation) suggesting the company can at least keep pace with tech titans pursuing rival platforms.

Yet, the broader industry is still pre‑product. Customers are experimenting, not operationalizing. Management’s model of shipping incrementally larger chips and exposing them through cloud integrators such as Amazon Braket hedges adoption uncertainty, but the strategy only pays if Rigetti can deliver error‑corrected performance before its cash pile runs thin.

Meanwhile, hyperscale incumbents like IBM and specialist peers IonQ and D‑Wave sprint down parallel paths, diluting any first‑mover edge. For investors, the stock therefore represents leveraged exposure to a transformative technology balanced by execution, funding, and adoption risks that could render outcomes binary. Until scientific milestones convert into a moat supported by sticky revenue, a hold rating is prudent.

Technology Roadmap

Rigetti’s R&D cadence has accelerated over the past 18 months. The company moved from its 84‑qubit Ankaa‑2 to Ankaa‑3 in under a year, folding in redesigned chip layouts that reduced crosstalk and bumped fidelity close to the 99% level many researchers view as the entry ticket to scalable error correction.

In collaboration with Riverlane, Rigetti recently demonstrated low‑latency feedback loops for quantum error correction on Ankaa‑2, a key proof‑of‑concept showing that its control stack can execute the fast cycles needed for logical qubits. While still far from production quality, the result narrows the gap with better‑funded rivals such as IBM, which targets a 1000‑plus qubit error‑corrected machine later this decade.

The next waypoint on Rigetti’s public roadmap calls for a >100‑qubit system with 99.5% median two‑qubit fidelity by year‑end 2025. If achieved, that milestone would push the architecture toward “utility‑scale” domains such as quantum chemistry. Failure, however, would hand valuable mindshare to trapped‑ion leader IonQ, which already claims commercial advantage for specific Monte‑Carlo workloads.

Commercial Traction

Revenue remains lumpy because most sales stem from milestone‑based government or research contracts. The UK National Quantum Computing Centre deal, which supplies a 24‑qubit system, exemplifies that mix: it provides predictable engineering deliverables but carries lower margins and limited follow‑on volume.

Rigetti’s cloud strategy offers broader reach. By exposing its processors through Amazon, Microsoft, and its own QCS platform, the firm can monetize compute cycles without costly direct‑sales teams. Yet, utilization remains thin; management has not disclosed meaningful usage metrics, and first‑quarter revenue per available qubit continues to drift lower as capacity outpaces demand.

Partnership announcements, such as a South Korean initiative with Norma to stand up an 84‑qubit cloud service, signal rising international interest but have yet to translate into backlog visibility. Until quantum advantage emerges for everyday workloads, Rigetti’s commercial flywheel will likely spin slowly.

Capital Position

Rigetti ended March 31 with $237.7 million of cash thanks to a February 2025 investment from Quanta Computer. On June 11 management opportunistically topped up liquidity through a $350 million “at‑the‑market” equity raise, lifting total cash to roughly $575 million and eliminating near‑term debt.

That runway supports at least two more processor generations under the current burn rate. Operating expenses of $22.1 million in the latest quarter imply annualized cash use of roughly $90 million, before capex and working‑capital swings. Absent revenue inflection, however, further dilution remains a material risk, particularly if macro conditions tighten risk capital for deep‑tech ventures.

Management has suggested that positive EBITDA could be possible once multiple utility‑class systems are sold annually, but even optimistic industry forecasts place that inflection no earlier than 2027. Investors therefore must weigh technological progress against ongoing dilution when sizing positions.

Financials

First‑quarter revenue dropped to $1.5 million from $3.1 million a year ago as milestone timing and fewer governmental projects weighed on top‑line comparisons. Gross margin remains volatile because most contracts bundle hardware and services; management did not provide an explicit figure, but commentary pointed to lower margins tied to the NQCC system.

Operating expenses rose 22% to $22.1 million, reflecting higher payroll and stock‑based compensation tied to expanded R&D and the Quanta partnership. Rigetti actually posted GAAP net income of $42.6 million, yet that headline profit was entirely due to a $62.1 million non‑cash gain from revaluing warrant and earn‑out liabilities; on an adjusted basis the company remained deep in the red.

Cash flow from operations was negative $18 million for the quarter after adjusting for the non‑cash derivatives gain, illustrating the persistent mismatch between scientific ambition and commercial traction. Full‑year 2024 revenue totaled only $10.6 million, down slightly year-on-year. Guidance for 2025 remains qualitative, emphasizing technical milestones rather than revenue targets.

Risks

Execution risk tops the list. Failure to deliver the >100‑qubit, 99.5%‑fidelity system by year‑end would undermine the claim that Rigetti can keep pace with larger rivals who enjoy broader fabs and bigger budgets.

Dilution is the second major concern. The June equity raise increased the share count above 300 million. If burn rates hold and revenue does not accelerate, another raise could arrive before 2027, pressuring per‑share economics.

Competitive intensity also looms large. IonQ (IONQ), with a $9.9 billion market cap and roughly $600 million of cash, is targeting $75–95 million of revenue this year (an order of magnitude above Rigetti) while D‑Wave promotes a gate‑model roadmap alongside its annealers. If those peers secure early commercial wins, Rigetti may struggle to remain on short‑lists despite technical parity.

Conclusion

At roughly 350x trailing‑twelve‑month sales, Rigetti’s equity clearly prices in an eventual payoff far beyond today’s lumpy contract revenue. IonQ trades at about 130x sales, while D‑Wave commands near 260x, leaving Rigetti the most expensive of the quantum pure plays despite lagging on commercial metrics.

Traditional multiples such as P/E, PEG, or EV/EBITDA are not meaningful because all three companies operate with negative earnings and cash burn. Investors therefore default to price‑to‑sales or enterprise‑value‑to‑research‑spend ratios, and on those measures Rigetti screens richly valued relative to peers with larger revenue bases and similar cash runways.

Quantum computing may yet transform entire industries, and Rigetti’s engineering progress keeps it in that race. Nevertheless, until the roadmap converts scientific milestones into recurring revenue and a moat, the shares look fully valued. The risk‑reward skew remains binary: quantum success could generate multibillion‑dollar upside, but missed technical or funding checkpoints could compress the multiple dramatically. On balance, a neutral/hold stance best reflects that asymmetry.

data.title: Valens Semiconductor: Undervalued Innovator With 200%+ Upside Potential In ADAS And Pro-AV
data.description:
Valens Semiconductor (NYSE: NYSE:VLN) has emerged as a noteworthy contender in the niche of high-speed connectivity semiconductors. Headquartered in Israel, the company has carved a reputation for delivering cutting-edge, proprietary DSP-based PHY layer technology that serves several demanding markets, including automotive, Pro-AV (professional audio-video), industrial machine vision, and medical technology. By leveraging its unique technology, Valens differentiates itself from larger competitors like Texas Instruments (TI) and Analog Devices (ADI).

Company Profile

Valens operates on a global scale, offering high-performance semiconductor chips renowned for their ability to transmit data over long distances with minimal latency and strong resistance to electrical noise. These capabilities are foundational to two key industry standards: HDBaseT and MIPI A-PHY, positioning the company as a crucial influencer in setting global connectivity benchmarks.

Technological Edge and Differentiation

The company’s key differentiator lies in its DSP-managed PHY layer, which allows for significantly better data integrity and immunity to electromagnetic interference (EMI) when compared to traditional analog solutions. Highlights of their technological advantage include:

  • Real-time, DSP-based noise cancellation, enhancing performance in high-EMI environments such as vehicles and industrial settings.
  • Capability to transmit multi-gigabit data using inexpensive, unshielded cables, effectively lowering overall system costs.
  • Zero-latency, uncompressed data transmission, which is especially valuable for applications like ADAS in cars, high-resolution medical imaging, and professional AV setups.

Superiority of Valens’ PHY Technology

Valens utilizes sophisticated DSP-driven PHY technology to achieve high-bandwidth data transfer (several gigabits per second) over distances up to 100 meters using standard cabling. This greatly exceeds the performance capabilities of standard Ethernet in terms of both distance and reliability. Their focus on uncompressed video transmission, critical for time-sensitive applications, offers a marked advantage. While some high-end AV systems might accept minimal compression, Valens’ no-compromise approach ensures optimal data fidelity.

In terms of electromagnetic compatibility (EMC), Valens’ chips consistently outperform automotive Ethernet counterparts, making them particularly attractive in the automotive sector. The company has labeled its victories in the automotive and industrial sectors as "knockout" wins, citing their success in stringent OEM testing as evidence of their technological leadership.

Robust DSP-Managed PHY Layer

The advanced PHY layer embedded in Valens chips enables gigabit-scale data transmission across affordable cables stretching as far as 100 meters (approximately 328 feet), far surpassing industry norms. The proprietary noise cancellation method demonstrates over 20 times greater EMI resilience than legacy analog approaches. Demonstrations involving A-PHY technology confirm that Valens maintains signal integrity even under extreme EMI conditions, where other systems fail—a crucial factor in safety-critical automotive applications.

Financial Health and Outlook

Valens is on a defined path of expansion, aiming to grow revenue from roughly $57 million in 2024 to between $220 million and $300 million by 2029. The company anticipates reaching EBITDA profitability once revenue surpasses the $110 million to $120 million mark. Here is a segment-wise financial breakdown:

  • Pro-AV: Expected to grow from $32 million (2024) to around $90–$100 million (2029), with gross margins between 65% and 75%.
  • Automotive: Forecasted to expand from $21 million (2024) to $65–$110 million (2029), with lower margins of roughly 35%, due to competitive pricing and specific market dynamics.
  • Industrial Machine Vision: Poised to grow from $1 million (2024) to $35–$50 million (2029), with estimated gross margins in the 55% to 65% range.
  • Medical: Still in the early stages, this segment has a total addressable market (TAM) of approximately $625 million, contingent upon widespread adoption of single-use endoscopes.

Valens experienced a peak in revenue at $91 million in 2022 after growing from $60 million in 2019. Revenue is expected to temporarily dip to about $57 million in 2024, attributed to post-COVID inventory normalization and customer budget adjustments.

Gross Margin Insights

Valens’ gross margins vary significantly across sectors: approximately 70% in Pro-AV and about 35% in automotive. The company anticipates maintaining a blended gross margin around 60% upon scaling, with operating expenses hovering near $80 million annually.

Revenue Evolution and Strategic Distribution

The company’s revenue mix is gradually diversifying:

  • In 2024: Pro-AV (~60%) and Automotive (~40%) dominate.
  • By 2029: Expected distribution is 45% Pro-AV, 32% Automotive, 18% Industrial Vision, and 2% from other sources. Medical remains a latent, long-term growth opportunity.

Competitive Landscape Review

Valens faces its primary competition in automotive connectivity from established players like TI and ADI. However, several differentiators put Valens in a strong position:

  • Technology Superiority: The DSP-managed PHY technology outperforms analog alternatives in both resilience and simplicity.
  • Cost Advantage: Valens' approach with unshielded cables results in significantly reduced system costs.
  • Standards Leadership: Unlike competitors, Valens plays a central role in shaping connectivity standards, particularly through MIPI A-PHY.

Key Growth Catalysts

Multiple forces are driving Valens' revenue outlook:

  • Automotive: Secured design wins with top OEMs including Mercedes-Benz, plus three additional European OEMs, and a strategic partnership with Mobileye to capitalize on ADAS opportunities.
  • Pro-AV Expansion: Increasing adoption of the VS6320 chipset, particularly for video conferencing in hybrid workplaces.
  • Industrial Vision: Rising demand driven by automation and AI-enhanced inspection workflows.
  • Strategic Partnerships: Collaborations with Acroname and ESWIN Computing are helping Valens deepen its market reach.

Segment-Specific Revenue Outlook (Source)

  • Pro-AV: Projected revenue of $90–100 million by 2029, driven by continued uptake of the VS6320 chip.
  • Industrial Machine Vision: Expected to expand from under $1 million to $35–50 million.
  • Automotive: Revenue predicted to reach $65–110 million, particularly as ADAS solutions scale in 2026 and beyond.

Opportunities in Each Market

  • Pro-AV: HDBaseT standard leadership and innovations like the VS6320 (USB 3.2 extension) position Valens well.
  • Automotive: Design wins and ADAS deployments with major OEMs support the $65–110 million forecast.
  • Industrial Machine Vision: The VA7000 chip addresses high-bandwidth, high-reliability needs for automation.
  • Medical: The single-use endoscopy market holds future promise, contingent on regulatory and clinical uptake.

Segment Financial Summary

Design Win Timelines

The typical cycle from evaluation to revenue generation:

Evaluation Phase:

  • Pro-AV: 6 months
  • Industrial: 6-12 months
  • Automotive: 12-18 months

Customer Development Phase:

  • Pro-AV: 6-9 months
  • Industrial: 9-12 months
  • Automotive: 24-30 months

Mass Production Phase:

  • Pro-AV: 3-5 years
  • Industrial: 5-7 years
  • Automotive: 5-7 years

Supply Chain Considerations

Valens operates in the high-end segment, which has shielded it from many supply chain disruptions. The company has never delayed customer production due to chip shortages, a testament to its reliability. That said, its heavy reliance on a few foundries—notably TSMC and Samsung—poses concentration risk. Chip redesigns for alternate foundries can take six months to two years, depending on whether the chip is digital or analog.

Valuation Methodologies and Target Price

Using a multipronged approach to valuation:

  • DCF Model: Utilized a 9% WACC, showing robust upside potential under both base and optimistic cases.
  • EV/EBITDA Model: Applied multiples in the 17x–23x range, yielding strong valuations.
  • P/E Analysis: Factored in EPS estimates and equity dilution, with P/E ratios from 24x to 28x.

Price Targets:

  • Base Case: $5.10/share (137% upside)
  • Best Case: $7.05/share (228% upside)

Long-Term Strategic Position and Risk Factors

Valens is well-positioned to tap into long-term trends, particularly in medical imaging and automotive ADAS. However, several risks persist:

  • Lengthy automotive design cycles
  • Supply chain concentration
  • Intense competition from well-established firms
  • Tariff and geopolitical tensions

Capital Strategy and Shareholder Alignment

The $25 million share repurchase program launched by Valens reflects management’s confidence in the company’s intrinsic value and future prospects. This signals prudent capital deployment and alignment with shareholder interests.

Conclusion

Valens Semiconductor presents a compelling case for investors looking to diversify within the semiconductor sector. With a clear technological edge, involvement in key industry standards, and a path toward scalable, diversified growth, Valens stands out as a high-potential investment opportunity. The company’s traction across multiple high-growth markets, paired with disciplined execution and thoughtful capital management, reinforces its long-term value proposition.
